channel.title: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins)
channel.description: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins) generated by script

item.title: Trump Seeks Help to Reopen Hormuz. Europe Says No.
item.description: After months of bashing allies, the White House finds itself fighting largely alone.

item.title: ‘A Moment of Grave Peril’
item.description: Aid agencies warn that the Iran war will deepen humanitarian crises across the region.

item.title: War may bring lasting change to the airline business
item.description: Western carriers will relish the chance to win some of their customers back

item.title: European Democracy Is Doing Just Fine, Top EU Official Says
item.description: Despite White House claims, the EU’s human rights official argues that “democracy is thriving” on the continent.

item.title: The quiet recovery of Ireland’s ancient tongue
item.description: In the land of St Patrick, Irish is making a steady comeback

item.title: Rapid-charging EV batteries are on the way
item.description: They can be topped up in as little time as a tank of fuel

item.title: Will America’s Asian allies get dragged into the Iran war?
item.description: They are worried Uncle Sam will abandon them if they don’t fight

item.title: The War Room newsletter: A conflict Trump was ill-prepared for
item.description: There is no simple way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A Maoist survival guide to the Iranian energy crisis
item.description: For a self-reliant power like China, no supplier is ever irreplaceable

item.title: The Demand Side of Our New Political Reality
item.description: Most commentaries on democratic erosion focus on the supply side of the equation – the strongmen and new doctrines, blocs, or geopolitical arrangements disrupting domestic politics and the rules-based international order. While important, this perspective ignores the demand that is driving current political trends.

item.title: BRICS Meets Reality in the Middle East War
item.description: It’s the latest case study in the persistent failure of transnational solidarity.

item.title: Jürgen Habermas, the Last Rationalist
item.description: The German philosopher built the 20th century’s most rigorous defense of democratic reason and then watched it tested.

item.title: Adam Smith on Today’s Global Economy
item.description: Two and a half centuries after its publication, The Wealth of Nations remains a useful lens for understanding the forces driving deglobalization, technological upheaval, and widening inequality. Far from championing unfettered capitalism, Smith would recognize markets’ limits and the need for oversight.

item.title: France’s Far Right Now Has a Clear Path to Power
item.description: Leftists and nationalists are managing to completely break republicanism.

item.title: The U.S. War on Iran Has Put the Lebanese Government in a Bind
item.description: It can neither protect Lebanon from Israel nor disarm Hezbollah.

item.title: The Feasibility Trap
item.description: It seems increasingly obvious that the behavior of each actor in the Iran war follows directly from what its technology allows it to do. The result is a dynamic in which military decisions are driven less by strategic calculation than by the operational possibilities created by intelligence and innovation.

item.title: Can Central Asia Cash in on the Critical Minerals Rush?
item.description: As the geopolitical race to secure alternative sources for critical minerals heats up, interest in Central Asia’s mining sector has kicked into a new gear.

item.title: North Korea Launches Massive Ballistic Missile Barrage Amid South Korea-US Drills
item.description: The unusual firing of 10 missiles comes as Pyongyang seeks to command attention while global focus remains fractured across multiple international conflicts.

item.title: Refashioning a Strategic Partnership: Canada and China in a Fragmenting World Order
item.description: The Canada-China breakthrough may have made things easier for other middle powers, like the U.K. and Germany, who have also stepped up their links with China recently.

item.title: Iran’s Escalation Strategy Won’t Work
item.description: Tehran’s compulsive gambling has paid off in the past, but this time looks different.

item.title: Pakistan’s Military Campaign in Afghanistan Is Here to Stay
item.description: Pakistan's recent strikes signal that it is prepared to take the fight directly to the Taliban leadership’s inner circle if the Taliban regime’s support for anti-Pakistan groups continues.

item.title: Gulf War 3.0: How Is India Securing Its Oil Supplies?
item.description: India’s limited reserves, thin domestic production capacity, and mounting oil import dependence leave it extremely vulnerable should the war in West Asia continue.

item.title: Iran’s Political Prisoners Are in the Firing Line
item.description: Thousands of detainees are in danger from U.S. and Israeli strikes and Tehran’s vengeance.

item.title: ‘Made in America’ Should Accept Chinese Investment
item.description: Private Chinese capital is being locked out of mutually beneficial opportunities.

item.title: The Hidden Economic Costs of Menopause
item.description: For too long, menopause was rarely discussed and understudied, leaving us with little knowledge of the economic and social costs associated with it. Now that the effects on productivity and public budgets are finally being measured, it is already clear that the long-term toll for society could be very large indeed.

item.title: Is the Ice Finally Breaking in Bangladesh-India Relations?
item.description: Security officials from the two countries met in Delhi days after the BNP government took charge in Dhaka.

item.title: The China–Iran Partnership and the Limits of Beijing’s Non-Interference Alliance Model
item.description: A preference for strategic flexibility has left Beijing dependent on a narrow set of "allies" whose usefulness is limited – especially in times of crisis.

item.title: ASEAN Again Calls for Halt to Iran War, Citing Possible ‘Sustained’ Economic Challenges
item.description: The bloc's economic ministers warned that a protracted conflict could hinder economic progress and impacting the livelihoods of millions across the region.

item.title: From Vietnam to Iran, War Is the Reason Americans Don’t Trust Their Government
item.description: Presidents cannot ignore the long-term costs of dismissing the truth in pursuit of national security.

item.title: How Takaichi Can Triumph
item.description: And what others can learn from Japan's embrace of American power.

item.title: Why Russia Is Watching Iran Burn
item.description: The Kremlin is in no hurry to save its closest partner in the Middle East.

item.title: Good Riddance to Corporate Social Responsibility
item.description: The end of an unnecessary fiction.

item.title: New Thai Parliament Convenes Amid Continuing Legal Scrutiny of February 8 Election
item.description: The Constitutional Court is set to rule on whether the use of barcodes and QR codes on ballot papers violated constitutional provisions on voter confidentiality.

item.title: Winners and Losers in the AI Workplace
item.description: With AI labs continuing to roll out new models and tools geared toward unlocking productivity gains in the workplace, the future of work has become a major economic, social, and political issue. The question, then, is which careers, occupations, and industries stand to gain and lose the most in the near term.

item.title: The Problem With Billionaires
item.description: There are compelling reasons why free, democratic societies should limit the amount of personal wealth any individual can have. Beyond the strong philosophical objections to extreme inequality, empirical research increasingly shows that economies, societies, and the planet would be better off without the ultra-rich.

item.title: How Inequality Caused America's Affordability Crisis
item.description: Recent election cycles have shown that “affordability” is among American voters’ greatest and most persistent concerns. But partly because the basic problem has been misdiagnosed, the widely appealing, commonsense solution to it has failed to gain political traction.

item.title: Lonely Empire
item.description: The Trump administration’s overt imperialism has unraveled the global order of shared norms and institutions faster than anyone expected. We now find ourselves at a juncture where everyone must either follow the US back into the jungle or refuse, leaving the America to its own devices.

item.title: PS Quarterly Interview: Desmond Lachman
item.description: Donald Trump is inadvertently inviting the return of bond-market vigilantes and a crisis in the US Treasury market – developments that could puncture the AI bubble, depress US equity prices, and tip the US economy into recession. But, from Japanese debt to Chinese imbalances, the global economy’s woes extend much further.

item.title: The Real Fallout of Trump’s Tariffs
item.description: Donald Trump might have hoped that his “Liberation Day” tariffs would punish China and boost the competitiveness of American industries, but they have instead had the opposite effect. Worse, the policy has had far-reaching unintended consequences that few policymakers and commentators have yet to reckon with.

item.title: Asian Manufacturing in Flux
item.description: Rising US-China frictions and worsening global imbalances have triggered a fundamental reordering of Asia’s manufacturing landscape. The ongoing redirection of trade and investment flows will likely hollow out traditional manufacturing in countries that fail to get ahead of the problem.

item.title: Money Enters a New Era
item.description: As recently as a century ago, private currencies competed with each other and with government-issued money. Not until the emergence of central banks did the balance shift decisively in favor of fiat currency; and now the pendulum is swinging back – though only partially, and not without introducing new risks.

item.title: Online Age-Gating Is the Wrong Answer
item.description: With social-media minimum-age restrictions – or “bans” – gaining traction globally, it is worth considering whether this popular new policy intervention is as effective as its proponents claim. The evidence suggests not, and even raises concerns that age-gating could do more harm than good.

item.title: Trumponomics Is Failing on Growth
item.description: After campaigning as a businessman who would usher in a new golden age of growth in America, US President Donald Trump's economic track record in 2017 proved disappointing. Despite large tax cuts and little standing in the way of Trump's deregulatory agenda, the US economy has performed no better under his administration than under the last.

item.title: The Bukele Effect in Latin America
item.description: Not surprisingly, Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele’s success in reducing violent crime has led other political leaders across the region to try to take a page from the same playbook. But offering the public a choice between insecurity and authoritarianism is not leadership; it is failure dressed up as resolve.

item.title: Global Markets Are A-Changin’
item.description: The top three trends shaping the global investment terrain suggest that risk assets will be the winners, at least in the short term. But investors should beware, because the same trends also threaten to alter the functionality of capital markets in multiple ways.

item.title: Why the Iran crisis caught Europe flat-footed
item.description: The EU suffers the war’s fallout, but can do little to cut it short

item.title: The Iran-Israel-US War Is Exposing China’s Alliance Problem
item.description: The conflict in the Middle East accentuates a structural weakness in China’s global strategy: the absence of capable allies that can act as a force multiplier.

item.title: China’s Difficult Choice in the Iran-Israel-US War
item.description: The development of the current conflict has placed Beijing in a complex and awkward strategic position.

item.title: The Iran war may be about to escalate
item.description: Gulf states could join the conflict

item.title: Donald Trump’s Iran war could hand Congress to the Democrats
item.description: As the conflict drags on, Democrats are sharpening their response

item.title: Trouble is brewing among America’s corporate borrowers
item.description: Surging energy prices will add to fears of a wave of defaults

item.title: Open-source intelligence shuts down
item.description: Satellite images of the Middle East are suddenly disappearing

item.title: What Nitish Kumar did for Bihar, India’s poorest state
item.description: The state’s longest-serving chief minister raised voters’ expectations of their politicians

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: An attack on the world economy
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s cover

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why America isn’t talking about the Iran war
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on a nation that is waiting and seeing

item.title: Trump Orders Strikes on Kharg Island, a Vital Hub for Iran’s Oil
item.description: However, Trump said that “for reasons of decency,” he chose “NOT to wipe out the Oil Infrastructure on the Island.”

item.title: Vladimir Putin enjoys a huge windfall from the Iran war
item.description: But the sugar high may not last

item.title: Trump’s China Trip: 3 Possible Deliverables
item.description: The United States and China need to lower expectations about Trump’s trip and focus on delivering a few concrete outcomes.

item.title: Skill at Scale: India’s Edge in Semiconductor Alliances
item.description: As Indo-Pacific powers race to reorganize semiconductor supply chains amid intensifying China-U.S. strategic competition, where does India fit in?

item.title: Six U.S. Troops Killed in Aircraft Crash in Iraq
item.description: Trump maintains that the United States is winning its war against Iran.

item.title: The Economic Costs of the Iran War, by the Numbers
item.description: From multimillion-dollar munitions to surging oil prices, here’s how much the war is costing.

item.title: China’s New Five-Year Plan Prioritizes Robotics. The World Should Pay Attention.
item.description: Beijing is embarking on a "whole-of-nation push" to achieve permanent dominance in physical AI technologies.

item.title: Iran War Puts India in Tricky Position
item.description: Modi’s government faces increasing domestic dissent when it comes to working with the Trump administration.

item.title: Gulf states are burning through interceptors
item.description: The pace far outstrips that seen in Ukraine. Stocks are under severe pressure

item.title: Gulf states are burning through interceptors
item.description: The pace far outstrips that seen in Ukraine. Stocks are under severe pressure

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of March 7: The war in Iran continues, conflict flares in South Sudan, and José Antonio Kast is inaugurated.

item.title: In Putin’s Russia, Resistance Is Lonely
item.description: An Oscar-nominated documentary offers an intimate view of Russia’s descent into fascism.

item.title: The Iran War Could Trigger a Global Food Crisis
item.description: While media coverage of Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz has focused on oil prices, the implications for global food supplies are no less alarming. A prolonged closure could disrupt agriculture worldwide and place more than 100 million people at risk of a humanitarian catastrophe.

item.title: Christian Democracy Is Facing Regime Change
item.description: Europe’s unique political tradition is under threat—and perfectly suited for the post-liberal moment.

item.title: Two Books About the Pull of Home
item.description: Plus, more international fiction releases in March.

item.title: Kashmir’s Endless Wait for Statehood – and Hope
item.description: Instead of working with locally elected officials in Jammu and Kashmir, New Delhi is drafting legislation to forcibly remove opposition figures from office.

item.title: Should you take GLP-1 drugs for longevity?
item.description: The evidence is tantalising. But that is not the same as proof

item.title: 5 Streaming Movies to Help Understand Iran Now
item.description: Many of these filmmakers have found themselves scrutinized by government censors, leading to arrests and imprisonment.

item.title: How the Climate Won Paris’s Culture War
item.description: Plenty of issues are on the ballot in this month’s mayoral election. The city’s green transition isn’t one of them.

item.title: The Iran-Israel-US War Is Reconfiguring US Force Posture in the Indo-Pacific
item.description: The concentration of U.S. military assets in the Middle East is forcing allies like Japan and South Korea to ask themselves hard questions about U.S. reliability.

item.title: Can Sierra Leone Still Keep Its Mothers Alive?
item.description: What foreign aid cuts mean in practice in one African country.

item.title: Is the Stablecoin Economy Structurally Sound?
item.description: If stablecoins and tokenized assets become systemically important, blockchains will become a form of systemically important infrastructure. But this shift will bring new risks, because not all blockchains are created equal, and they have largely avoided the degree of scrutiny the public expects of critical infrastructure.

item.title: What China’s Five-Year Plan Says About Its Economy
item.description: A growth target of 4.5 percent is the lowest in decades.

item.title: Despite Top-level Tensions, Strategic Mineral Cooperation Among Western Allies Is Quietly Deepening
item.description: The effort to reduce dependence on Chinese-controlled supply chains is now starting to have tangible results.

item.title: Shockwaves Across Asia: The Iran War’s Strategic Fallout
item.description: From energy disruptions to alliance tensions, a geographically distant conflict is having immediate consequences across the Indo-Pacific.

item.title: Mobilizing Africa’s Capital for African Development
item.description: From growing pools of African savings to expanding global sovereign wealth funds, there is more than enough capital available to close Africa's funding gaps. To mobilize it effectively, development banks must start thinking less like balance-sheet lenders and more like "capital architects."

item.title: Vietnam and the Geopolitics of Critical Minerals
item.description: While Washington builds coalitions, Vietnam is betting on self-reliance.

item.title: India Can Avoid the Middle-Income Trap
item.description: Can India avoid the dreaded middle-income trap that has ensnared so many other developing countries in Latin America and Southeast Asia? Its favorable demographics, steadily improving economic governance and civil administration, and recent trade agreements certainly suggest so.

item.title: Japan’s Political Paradox: Why Young Liberals Backed a Conservative Landslide
item.description: Are young Japanese voting based on an “idol effect” – or are they redefining entirely what it means to be liberal vs conservative?

item.title: South Koreans Keep the Faith in America Even as Washington Tests It
item.description: South Koreans continue to choose the United States over any rival power by historically wide margins. But don't call it naivety.

item.title: Trump’s Summit Offers More Deference Than Details
item.description: Leaders emerged from the “Shield of the Americas” with few policy specifics.

item.title: Why India Is Right to Support the US and Israel in the Iran War
item.description: What good would it do India to hitch itself to the sinking ship of Iran, instead of its actual partners, such as Israel and the UAE?

item.title: The Gulf’s Tough Choices
item.description: For the Gulf Cooperation Council's six member states, strategic patience may be the wisest approach to the Iran war for now. But the longer the war goes on, the more the region’s credibility as a stable global hub erodes – a vulnerability that the Islamic Republic is determined to exploit.

item.title: Lessons From China’s Age-Gated Internet
item.description: Policymakers worldwide are debating the merits of age-gating and strict content controls to protect children online. China’s experience with these tactics shows that even the most intrusive safeguards cannot reliably prevent harm, which suggests that they are not worth the trade-off in civil liberties.

item.title: How the Iran War Could Usher in a New Era of Global Shiite Extremism
item.description: Degrading command and control over proxy groups could make them more dangerous and unpredictable.

item.title: The Spheres-of-Influence Illusion
item.description: For the first time since 1945, the United States, China, and Russia are converging around an authoritarian conception of sovereignty that relies on raw power rather than international law. But history suggests that dividing the world into rival blocs is more likely to produce conflict than stability.

item.title: A New Global Energy Shock
item.description: When the United States and Israel launched their war of choice against Iran, the Islamic Republic pursued the most effective response at its disposal: triggering a spike in global energy prices by attacking Gulf energy infrastructure and blocking the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. While the long-term effects on energy markets remain difficult to predict, US President Donald Trump has good reason to seek an off-ramp from a war opposed by most Americans, including his MAGA base. But with Israel committed to “total victory” and a defiant Islamic Republic now mining the strait, a cascade of global economic disruption appears increasingly likely.

item.title: The Big Picture
Indonesia’s Local Content Requirements Are No Shortcut to Industrialization
item.description: Without more accountability and transparency, LCRs will remain a blunt protectionist instrument rather than a true catalyst for industrial transformation.

item.title: Major Southeast Asian Economies Targeted by New US Trade Probes
item.description: Against the backdrop of the war in Iran, the move is likely to create new points of friction in U.S. relations with the region.

item.title: Why Europe’s Approach to Defending Democracy Is Failing
item.description: Bromides about the “center holding” miss the point.

item.title: The Hormuz Minefield
item.description: In the strait, Iran holds the advantage—and America has no good options.

item.title: The New Khamenei
item.description: How America and Israel solved Iran’s succession problem.

item.title: How Latin America Failed Venezuela
item.description: The region’s divisions helped precipitate U.S. intervention.

item.title: Matthew Kroenig and Trita Parsi Debate the War in Iran
item.description: Will the campaign advance U.S. interests in the region or set them back?

item.title: Strategic Stakes Rise for Bangladesh as Iran Targets Gulf Neighbors
item.description: There are practical ways in which Bangladesh could contribute to the security and stability of the Gulf without becoming directly involved in military confrontation.

item.title: Indonesia and Australia to Expand Security Cooperation to Include Japan, Papua New Guinea
item.description: The announcement follows the signing of a major security agreement between Jakarta and Canberra last month.

item.title: Iran’s Strait of Hormuz Toolkit: Drones, Missiles, and Mines
item.description: A breakdown of the tools Iran has to maintain pressure on Trump.

item.title: Iran’s New Supreme Leader Vows to Continue Blocking the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran escalates Gulf strikes and warns of future attacks on U.S. bases in the region.

item.title: How the New Gulf War Is Impacting Mongolia
item.description: Countries like Mongolia, despite being distant from the conflict, are already feelings its impact on their economic activities, not to mention how the war disrupts future possibilities.

item.title: Trump’s Last-Gasp Foreign Policy
item.description: Ill-considered overreach in Iran and elsewhere signals imminent decline.

item.title: Trump’s Efforts to Defuse the Oil Spike Aren’t Working
item.description: Big reserve releases, promises of escorts, and insurance can’t convince the market that the crisis will be over soon.

item.title: A Film of Unimaginable Horror and Unrelenting Humanity
item.description: ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab’ is an indictment of the international system that has failed Gaza.

item.title: Deterring China: The Growing Japan–Philippines Security Partnership
item.description: The growing partnership between Japan and the Philippines is not an actual alliance – but it could affect the balance of power in the South China Sea.

item.title: China’s High-Tech Narrative Cannot Solve Its Deflation Problem
item.description: For all the emphasis on developing advanced technologies, China’s present growth model still depends far more on low-cost manufactured exports.

item.title: South Korea’s Conservative Party Tanks Amid Warring Factions’ Fight for Legitimacy
item.description: The PPP ruined its local elections prospects by purging Han Dong-hoon, its former leader.

item.title: How to (Not) Be a Pacific Power
item.description: Washington has yet to recognize that mobility is an essential part of regional security.

item.title: How wars are adding hours to your flights
item.description: Our analysis shows which routes are getting longer

item.title: How China’s AI-Powered Robots Could Reshape the Global Order
item.description: China is moving from AI in the cloud to “embodied intelligence” with physical, real-world applications.

item.title: How to Electrify the Global South
item.description: Rather than rely on incremental efforts or one-size-fits-all models, countries hoping to achieve rapid progress on electrification must integrate distributed and centralized systems, make the most of AI-enabled tools, and send credible signals that attract private capital. India and Nigeria have shown how this can be done.

item.title: India’s Supreme Court Grants Bail to Veteran Kashmiri Separatist Shabir Ahmed Shah
item.description: The justices cited prolonged pre-trial detention exceeding six years, procedural irregularities in the trial process, and the slow pace of witness examination.

item.title: China is wrestling with a novel phenomenon: inherited wealth
item.description: Even as the economy slows and opportunity narrows, a lucky few receive big windfalls

item.title: Haiti needs order first, then elections
item.description: Voters must be able to turn out without risking death

item.title: How to teach Donald Trump a Latin lesson
item.description: By alienating Hispanics, he has given Democrats an open goal

item.title: China’s hereditary elite is taking shape
item.description: The Communist Party is afraid to tax inherited wealth

item.title: Islamists woo Bangladesh with everything but Islam
item.description: They are a hit on campus, too

item.title: Taiwan’s bid to export drones free of Chinese parts is taking off
item.description: The island’s unmanned aerial vehicles are already in use on Ukraine’s front line

item.title: Nepal’s new prime minister is a 35-year-old former rapper
item.description: Voters have given Balendra Shah an unprecedented mandate and enormous power

item.title: Seoul’s housing market is a huge political and economic headache
item.description: Lee Jae Myung is staking his presidency on fixing it

item.title: China’s nationalist spy thriller has few girls and lots of government
item.description: “Main melody” films are in a rut

item.title: Hong Kong’s property market has turned
item.description: That’s not good news for everyone

item.description: It may help them get promotions

item.description: If the Iran war goes wrong, MAGA will want a culprit who is not named Donald Trump

item.description: The regime’s enforcers will struggle to rebuild their capabilities and their deterrence

item.description: No one knows if he will last

item.description: Markets always struggle to price technological revolutions

item.description: AI promises to eliminate drudgery. Careful what you wish for

item.title: Altman, Amodei and Musk fight dirty for the biggest prize in business
item.description: Never before has so much money been sought from investors in a single year

item.title: Brazilian cinema is having its moment
item.description: Thank growing competition and a more internationally minded Academy awards

item.title: Iran’s praetorian guard may leave the war diminished but undefeated
item.description: Even if it will struggle to rebuild its capabilities

item.title: The Iran War’s Muddled Endgame
item.description: While Donald Trump has yet to articulate clear goals for the war in Iran, he would prefer to limit the energy shock and avoid a prolonged military engagement. But Israel does not share his reservations, and Iran views the current conflict as existential, so putting the genie back in the bottle will be no easy feat.

item.title: The Iran Crisis Is Fueling South Korea’s Drive for Nuclear Energy
item.description: Amid the Middle East conflict, South Korea is determined to find new pathways to strengthen localized, “autonomous” energy solutions by targeting civil nuclear and renewable energy.

item.title: India Is Not the 4th Biggest Economy in the World. But Does That Really Matter?
item.description: The nationalist narrative that dominated public discourse over the past year had been overstating the scale of India’s economic progress. Of particular concern is uneven and inequitable growth.

item.title: The War That Cracked the Postwar Order Illusion
item.description: Conflict in Ukraine has shattered the myths sustaining the Western coalition.

item.title: The American Narrative About a Revisionist China Is Upside-Down
item.description: The United States has become the dangerous revisionist power it long warned us that China would be.

item.title: Infrastructure Investment Is the Key to China’s Growth
item.description: China’s government has now set its growth target for 2026 at 4.5-5%. With China’s GDP deflator still in negative territory and ample policy space for more expansionary fiscal and monetary policy, this is achievable, but it will require boosting both consumption and investment spending.

item.title: Regional Crises Are Dealing a Heavy Blow to Afghanistan’s Fragile Economy
item.description: For a country whose economy remains deeply dependent on regional trade routes, migration, and external assistance, instability on multiple fronts can quickly translate into economic crisis.

item.title: The Iran War Is Now Impacting AUKUS
item.description: The HMS Anson’s abrupt departure from Australia shows the limits of Britain’s stretched fleet. That will have major implications for AUKUS.

item.title: Beijing Is Legalizing the Assimilation of Tibetans and Other Ethnic Minorities
item.description: China is amending its laws to weaken ethnic identities, including Tibetan language and culture.

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: How baseball created 20th-century America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explores the making of a national pastime in an age of immigration and industrialisation

item.title: Moving Up, Locked In: The Hidden Costs of ASEAN’s Semiconductor Push
item.description: As the region moves up the chip value chain – toward AI development, advanced logic, and high-bandwidth memory – U.S. regulatory pressures increase.

item.title: Scotland Is Pointing the Way to a New Economy
item.description: As more people worldwide confront economic failures and climate disruptions, systemic change will become increasingly more attractive. Scotland’s new Community Wealth Building Bill can serve as an example of a policy framework that promotes broad-based ownership, collective decision-making, and the reconfiguration of economic power.

item.title: America’s war on Iran may bring Bahrain to its knees
item.description: The tiny, indebted kingdom is seeing its main sources of revenue dry up

item.title: The damage to the world economy from the Iran war will be severe, but uneven
item.description: It has already caused the biggest energy supply shock in history

item.title: The Growing Cyber Risk to Supply Chains
item.description: Many corporate leaders regard cybersecurity as an internal IT problem that can be delegated and forgotten. But as AI and automation reshape global supply chains, cyber readiness has become an operational capability, similar to quality or safety, with the goal being continuity in the face of disruption.

item.title: NATO and the IP4: Potential and Limitations
item.description: The growing attention to NATO’s Indo-Pacific partners reflects both strategic necessity and political uncertainty.

item.title: In Trump’s world, companies seek insurance against political risk
item.description: It could soon get more expensive

item.title: An attack on the world economy
item.description: Whatever happens in the Strait of Hormuz, energy markets have been changed for ever

item.title: There are no good options for Iran’s nuclear programme
item.description: If America cannot eliminate the threat, what should it do?

item.title: The Irresistible Urge to Invoke World War III
item.description: Forget the inflated comparisons. Neither the war in the Middle East nor the Russian invasion of Ukraine ever signaled a global conflagration.

item.title: Consumers or Workers First?
item.description: A well-functioning economy, say most economists, is one that provides a widening array of ever-more affordable goods and services. But what we need is an economic-policy mindset that recognizes that people are both consumers and workers.

item.title: Inside Hong Kong’s Cage Homes: The Elderly Residents on the Frontlines of the Heat Crisis

item.title: The D Brief: US-Israel strikes continue; Hormuz closed; 4 US dead, ID’d; SOF join Ecuador drug ops; And a bit more.
Nuclear Expansion and Conditional Diplomacy: North Korea’s New Strategy
item.description:
At the 9th Congress of the Workers’ Party of Korea, held every five years, North Korea’s Kim Jong Un left the door open for engagement with the U.S. and reaffirmed hostility with South Korea.

The seven-day congress replaced over 40% of senior officials with younger loyalists, including Mr. Kim’s sister, Kim Yo Jong, promoted to General Affairs Director of the Central Committee. A new five-year economic plan emphasized self-reliance and national development. It included ambitious targets for grain production and industrial output, emphasizing ideological cohesion.

The congress codified North Korea’s status as a permanent nuclear-armed state, emphasizing further nuclear expansion and modernization. It also stated that North Korea’s conventional forces would be upgraded.

A new concept referred to as “Haekpangasoe” (nuclear trigger) was formalized and according to the Korean Central News Agency it is an integrated nuclear crisis response system designed to ensure that the national nuclear shield could be operated promptly and accurately at any moment, ensuring responsiveness to perceived threats. Thus, North Korea’s nuclear deterrent now includes a pre-emptive nuclear strike capability.

North Korea formally reaffirmed hostility toward South Korea, designating it the “first hostile state” and institutionalized severed ties, effectively discarding their long-standing policy of reunification. This was North Korea doubling down on their “two hostile states” doctrine.

While clearly emphasizing antagonism toward South Korea, Mr. Kim suggested openness to talks with Washington, but only if the U.S. accepts North Korea’s nuclear status and abandons its “hostile policy” toward North Korea.

There is speculation that President Donald Trump’s scheduled visit to China in April to meet with China’s President Xi Jinping would be an opportune time for Mr. Trump to again meet with Mr. Kim, this time in Beijing or Pyongyang. Their initial meeting in Singapore in 2018 was relatively successful, with a joint statement committing both countries to establish a new relationship in accordance with the desire of the peoples of the two countries for peace and prosperity, with North Korea committing to work towards complete denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. The 2019 summit in Hanoi failed when Mr. Trump ended the meeting abruptly when Mr. Kim would only include his plutonium reactor at Yongbyon in exchange for the lifting of sanctions. A brief symbolic meeting at the DMZ later in 2019 did not include a substantive discussion of sanctions relief for denuclearization.

Much has happened since North Korea’s 8th Party Congress in January 2021. Complete and verifiable denuclearization is now off the table. In 2022, North Korea had officially enshrined the right to use pre-emptive nuclear strikes to protect itself from an imminent or perceived to be an imminent attack. The 9th Party Congress has now memorialized the Haekpangasoe nuclear trigger doctrine, ensuring that nuclear weapons can and will be used in response to perceived threats. And South Korea, despite efforts of the Lee Jae-Myung government to improve relations with North Korea, is viewed by North Korea as their “most hostile entity”, renouncing reunification and institutionalizing the “two hostile states” doctrine, while keeping the door open to another summit with Mr. Trump, if the U.S. accepts North Korea as a nuclear weapons state and discontinues its “hostile policy” toward North Korea.

Equally important is the June 2024 comprehensive strategic partnership between North Korea and Russia and their mutual defense treaty, with North Korea providing over 12,000 troops to support Russia in its war of aggression against Ukraine. In addition to troops, North Korea is also providing Russia with artillery shells and ballistic missiles and reportedly receiving missile and nuclear assistance from Russia.

The situation on the Korean Peninsula is tense, with a nuclear North Korea, aligned with Russia and China, viewing South Korea as the enemy. And given North Korea’s emphasis on tactical nuclear weapons and its doctrine dealing with the pre-emptive use of nuclear weapons, efforts to deescalate tension on the Korean Peninsula are necessary.

Mr. Kim’s comment at the 9th Party Congress that North Korea could “get along” with the U.S. -- and apparent willingness to again meet with Mr. Trump – could be the only viable option for defusing the potential for conflict on the Korean Peninsula.

Another meeting with Mr. Kim does not mean the U.S. has to accept North Korea as a nuclear weapons state. It means we don’t have to put denuclearization up front in our discussions with North Korea. We do, however, must talk about sanctions relief, economic development assistance, peaceful nuclear energy and a path to normal relations when there is progress on human rights in North Korea. And in return, North Korea will have to agree to a moratorium on nuclear tests, fissile material production for nuclear weapons, and medium and long-range ballistic missile launches. Eventually, denuclearization on the Korean Peninsula will have to be discussed, but that could be after a decade or longer of negotiations.

Another summit between the leaders of the U.S. and North Korea is in the interest of peace and stability in East Asia.

Joseph R. DeTrani

The author is a former associate director of national intelligence. All statements of fact, opinion or analysis expressed are those of the author and do not reflect the official positions or views of the U.S. government. Nothing in the contents should be construed as asserting or implying U.S. government authentication of information or endorsement of the author’s views.

This piece by Cipher Brief Expert Ambassador Joe Detrani was first published by The Washington Times
